coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of multicolor carbon dots at room temperature and in atmosphere toward energy-efficient preparation and LEDs application
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Guihua
Shen, Jie
Zhang, Liguo
Lin, Liangliang
Zheng, Xujing
Xu, Xuechun
Du, Pengfei
Xu, Hujun
coredata.dc:description: In the reaction conditions of room temperature and atmosphere, the synthesis of carbon dots (CDs), especially those with multicolor, is of great potential since it allows for large-scale production. Herein, the multicolor CDs (MCDs) with blue, green, yellow and red colors were synthesized in one step at room temperature and in atmosphere without any external energy. Using o-phenylenediamine (o-PDA) as a precursor, N, N-Dimethylformamid (DMF) as solvent, the addition of different oxidants (HNO3, H2O2) and various polyol or polybasic acids makes the MCDs emitting diverse fluorescence colors. Based on structural characterization, the formation mechanism of MCDs can be explained that the energy released from oxidation, Schiff base and amindation reactions promotes dehydration, self-polymerization and carbonation, leading to MCDs successfully synthesized. The optical mechanism derived from both experimental and theoretical analyses demonstrates that particle size, graphitization and conjugated systems influence the emission wavelength of MCDs. Finally, the MCDs were successfully applied in down-converting LEDs with satisfied properties. This study provides an effective strategy to prepare MCDs with the concept of “energy-efficient chemistry” and potentially realizes industrial production.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Multicolor carbon dots
Room temperature
Atmosphere
Formation and optical mechanisms
Down-converting LEDs application

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and optical properties of MCDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a-d) The ultraviolet-visible absorption, photoluminescence excitation, and photoluminescence emission spectra of B-CDs, G-CDs, Y-CDs, and R-CDs were analyzed. Insets depict photographs of MCDs in DMF exposed to sunlight (left) and ultraviolet light (excited at 365 nm) (right). e-h) B-CDs, G-CDs, Y-CDs, and R-CDs excitation-emission matrices, respectively.

a-d) The morphology of B-CDs, G-CDs, Y-CDs, and R-CDs are displayed by TEM images. e-h) The lattice structure of B-CDs, G-CDs, Y-CDs, and R-CDs are displayed by HRTEM and FFTs (inset) images. i-l) The particle size distribution of B-CDs, G-CDs, Y-CDs, and R-CDs. m) XRD patterns MCDs n) The degree of graphitization of MCDs were analyzed by Raman spectroscopy. o) FTIR spectroscopy is used to study the structures of MCDs.
a-d) The structures of B-CDs, G-CDs, Y-CDs, and R-CDs were characterized by their XPS full survey spectrum and high-resolution XPS C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s spectra.
a) Molecular structure of the MCDs by MS. b) Scheme of the synthesis process of the MCDs.
a) Schematic of tunable emission from MCDs. b-e) B-CDs, G-CDs, Y-CDs, and R-CDs structural models and LUMO/HUMO calculations at the B3LYP/6–31 g(d) level.

a) MCDs/PVP films. b-e) CIE color coordinates of B-CDs, G-CDs, Y-CDs and R-CDs, respectively. (Inset: down-converting LED applications illustration). f-i) B-CDs, G-CDs, Y-CDs and R-CDs emission spectra.
A diagram of MCDs synthesis and their application in LEDs.
Schematic illustration for the formation of MCDs.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of multicolor carbon dots at room temperature and in atmosphere toward energy-efficient preparation and LEDs application

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the reaction conditions of room temperature and atmosphere, the synthesis of carbon dots (CDs), especially those with multicolor, is of great potential since it allows for large-scale production. Herein, the multicolor CDs (MCDs) with blue, green, yellow and red colors were synthesized in one step at room temperature and in atmosphere without any external energy. Using o-phenylenediamine (o-PDA) as a precursor, N, N-Dimethylformamid (DMF) as solvent, the addition of different oxidants (HNO3, H2O2) and various polyol or polybasic acids makes the MCDs emitting diverse fluorescence colors. Based on structural characterization, the formation mechanism of MCDs can be explained that the energy released from oxidation, Schiff base and amindation reactions promotes dehydration, self-polymerization and carbonation, leading to MCDs successfully synthesized. The optical mechanism derived from both experimental and theoretical analyses demonstrates that particle size, graphitization and conjugated systems influence the emission wavelength of MCDs. Finally, the MCDs were successfully applied in down-converting LEDs with satisfied properties. This study provides an effective strategy to prepare MCDs with the concept of “energy-efficient chemistry” and potentially realizes industrial production.

The synthesis of multicolor carbon dots in room temperature and in atmosphere and the illustration of formation mechanism
.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As a new kind of photoluminescence nano-material, carbon dots (CDs) possess various captivating properties, including tunable fluorescence [1], easy functionalization [2,3], good chemical stability [4] and low toxicity [5]. Therefore, they are widely applied in optoelectronics [6,7], biological imaging [8,9], fluorescence sensors [10], medical therapy [11] and catalysts [12]. Due to their remarkable properties and potential applications, the preparation of CDs has become a research hotspot. Up to now, there are many methods, such as discharge [13], microwave treatment [14,15], electrochemical exfoliation [16], hydrothermal and solvothermal synthesis [17–19]. However, these methods confront some problems that hinder large-scale production. Most of these methods require huge amounts of energy, including high temperature and even high pressure [20–22]. For example, as the most popular method, hydrothermal synthesis often requires the temperature between 150 and 230 °C under high pressure, probably leading to a risk to safety. Moreover, the equipment for these methods is very complex and expensive. Some methods are tedious and have many side reactions, especially when using high energy, resulting in low efficiency. In addition, these methods are often performed in a sealed environment, making it difficult to monitor and control the reaction processes, such as polymerization and carbonization. As a result, it is a considerable challenge to propose a reasonable strategy for CDs preparation with easy equipment, low energy, high production, adjustable process and low cost, which agrees well with “energy-efficient chemistry”.

In recent years, the CDs preparation at room temperature and in atmosphere has received a great deal of interest [23–25]. Different from the previous methods above, this method not only saves a large amount of energy but also has a simple operation process. Moreover, the equipment is so facile that this method can be potentially applied in large-scale preparation. Hou et al. [26] used acetaldehyde to generate CDs at room temperature and pressure by aldol condensation. During the reaction, nitrogen and nitrogen/sulfur were provided by carbamide and cysteine to confirm that heteroatom-doped CDs can be easily prepared under this condition. Lu et al. [27] used o-PDA as the unique precursor and employed hydrogen peroxide as an oxidant to synthesize CDs at room temperature in air. After silica column chromatography and acid treatment, full color CDs from blue, green to red were achieved. Wang et al. [28] dissolved o-PDA in ethanol for three days to harvest polymer dots, followed by carbonation with concentrated sulfuric acid to generate red emissive CDs. It is the first time to separate polymerization and carbonation processes, which makes it possible to control the intermediates and understand the formation of CDs. It is apparent that the CDs preparation at room temperature and in air has made prominent progress. Nevertheless, most CDs only have short emission wavelengths like blue or green, limiting further application [29]. Moreover, too long reaction times, low yields and unsatisfactory properties hinder the CDs in large-scale preparation at room temperature and in atmosphere [25,28]. In addition, multicolor CDs (MCDs) prepared at room temperature and in air are rarely reported. From the current literature [27], the room temperature synthesized MCDs were totally obtained by follow-up column chromatography, making the process more laborious and inefficient, including product and solvent loss. Since MCDs are considered a very potential material due to their tunable optical properties and subsequently enlarged applications [30,31], it makes sense to develop a facile and efficient pathway to synthesize MCDs at room temperature and pressure.

The reagent grade of o-PDA is purchased from Adamas. DMF, nitric acid, hydrochloric acid and H2O2 are provided by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). All chemical reagents are used without further purification. Deionized water with a conductivity of 1.82 iMΩ cm−1 was obtained from the Millipore purification system.

preparation of Blue-emissive CDs (B-CDs): 1.08 g of o-PDA and 1.80 g of glucose were dispersed in 50 mL of DMF in a beaker, then 8 mL of HNO3 was added in the mixture drop by drop with continuously stirring at room temperature in atmosphere. After reacting for 6 h, the mixture was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 30 minutes to take out large insoluble particles and filtered using a microporous membrane (Weight cutoff: 500 Da) of 0.22 μm. At last, the solution was dialyzed for 2 days and then freeze-dried to collect B-CDs powder.
Preparation of Green-emissive CDs (G-CDs): Except for the precursors substituted with o-PDA and ascorbic acid, the preparation process was identical to that of B-CDs.
Preparation of Yellow-emissive CDs (Y-CDs): Except for the precursors substituted with o-PDA and malic acid, the preparation process was identical to that of B-CDs.

Preparation of Red-emissive CDs (R-CDs): R-CDs were produced under the identical conditions as B-CDs, except for the precursors and oxidants. The reaction precursors are o-PDA and citric acid, and the oxidant is 8 mL of H2O2. Finally, 8 mL of HCl was added to the solution.

In 5 mL of DMF, 0.5 mg of CDs was combined with 5 g of PVP-K90 to make the blue/green/yellow/red fluorescent glass films. The mixture was mixed until it was uniform and transparent. The mixture was then spun-coated onto a clean glass sheet and cured for one day at 80 °C.

LEDs were made using UV-LED chips with excitation wavelengths at 365 nm. The CDs/PVP films were ground into powder, which was then combined with ET-821 A silica (0.8 g) and ET-821 B silica gel (0.2 g). The concoction was slathered on top of the chips.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and optical properties of MCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The reaction time was taken into account to investigate the synthetic process (Figure S1-S2). Under 365 nm UV irradiation, the first cuvette in each image, respectively, emits purple, blue, purple and yellow light within 1 min. Although most of these lights are trivial, it is indicated that the MCDs begin to be synthesized. the color of the four MCDs become deep under sunlight after reacting for 6 h. In the meantime, the four MCDs, respectively, emit blue, green, yellow and red light under ultraviolet radiation. Meanwhile, the intensities of emission wavelength of the four MCDs obviously strengthen when reacting for 6 h and keep stable at 8 h, revealing that the MCDs are completely formed within 6 h at room temperature and in atmosphere. For MCDs, the emission wavelength red-shifts as reaction time grows. Particularly for B-CDs, the peak at 575 nm disappears when the reaction time exceeds 6 h. These variations above may be attributed to the newly generated luminescent center during the reaction. Meanwhile, the UV–vis spectra of the four MCDs further support this interpretation in another aspect. The absorption peak of B-CDs moves from 290 nm to 330 nm as reaction time increases, suggesting the transfer of π-π* transition to n-π* transition. The extended reaction time for G-CDs and Y-CDs contributes to a new absorption peak at 450 nm, corresponding to n-π* transition. Furthermore, two additional absorption bands located at 350 nm and 450 nm can be observed in the spectrum of R-CDs. It is suggested that the absorption peak above 400 nm stands for extensive sp2 structure [32,33], which is crucial to the luminescent center and fluorescence intensity of CDs.

The oxidant content of MCDs during the reaction was investigated (Figure S3). The QYs of MCDs increase with the ratio of oxidant to o-PDA up to 0.8 (v/n: mL/mmol) and then remain stable. It is shown that this content of oxidant makes the reaction carry out sufficiently. In other words, the lack of oxidants leads to the low yield of MCDs. The effect of molar ratio on QYs was explored, showing that an equal molar ratio of the two reactants contributes to the highest QYs. This is because the less o-PDA couldn’t provide appropriate rigid π-π structures. On the other hand, the excess o-PDA leads to insufficient dehydration and carbonation, reducing the quality of MCDs. DMF not only serves as solution but also partly takes part in the reaction, which will be discussed in the following section. The addition of DMF in the systems was studied. As the ratio of o-PDA to DMF is 0.2 (n/v: mmol/mL), the QYs of the four MCDs achieved the maximum.

The luminescence characteristics of the four MCDs were displayed in Fig. 1a-d. The maximum emission wavelengths of the four MCDs are 490 nm, 530 nm, 570 nm and 605 nm. The quantum yields calculated by the reference method are 32.3% (B-CDs), 26.8% (G-CDs), 22.6% (Y-CDs) and 13.5% (R-CDs). Moreover, the overlaps between exciton absorptions and emission wavelengths of MCDs are slight, indicating low self-absorption and effective fluorescence emission [34]. Fig. 1e-h depicts the excitation-emission matrix. It is revealed that all the MCDs exhibit excitation-independent emission, suggesting a uniform fluorescence emission center [34–36].

Fig. 1a-d describes the multiple absorption transitions of MCDs in UV–vis spectra. The peaks below 300 nm and above 330 nm, respectively, represent pronounced π-π* absorptions and n-π* absorptions. The observed vibrational structures are attributed to π-π* specific electron energy levels [37]. B-CDs has a unique absorption band at 331 nm, responsible for π-π* transition of CC. Unlike B-CDs, the other three MCDs contain one more absorption band at about 450 nm, which is n-π* transition of conjugated bonds such as CN and CO [38]. It is proposed that the absorption peak at more than 400 nm means the conjugated sp2 system. That is probably the reason why the other three MCDs have longer emission wavelengths than B-CDs.

As displayed in TEM images ( Fig. 2a-d), the MCDs show a spherical shape and are well-dispersed and homogenous. The average sizes continuously increase as the color of MCDs alters from blue to red (Fig. 2i-l). This indicates that the longer emission wavelength corresponds to the larger particle size [39]. HRTEM images (Figs. 2e-h, S4) imply that the four kinds of MCDs have the same lattice structure, of which interplanar spacing is 0.21 nm, corresponding to (100) crystallographic facet of graphene. FFT patterns (Fig. 2 e-h) indicate MCDs possess the same single crystal plane (100), which is consistent with that from HRTEM images [37]. XRD patterns are exhibited in Fig. 2m. A wide band around 25 ° is observed in all MCDs, which is attributed to (002) plane of graphene structure with d spacing of 0.34 nm. This interlayer stacking in MCDs is extremely similar to that in graphitic structures (d=0.35 nm). It is further proposed that highly conjugated sp2 domains are closely packed in the carbon core [40]. Moreover, a small peak at 19.8 ° is observed in R-CDs. This peak corresponds to (002) plane of graphite with d spacing of 0.41 nm. The enlarged interlayer space means the surface of R-CDs has more functional groups. Based on the width of the diffraction peak, the CDs with a more red-shifted color possess better crystallization [38]. The Raman spectrum (Fig. 2 n) exhibits two distinct peaks at 1355 cm−1 and 1580 cm−1. The two peaks above are respectively assigned to the disordered D band and the crystalline G band. The intensity ratios (IG/ID) of B-CDs to R-CDs are respectively 1.03, 1.06, 1.25 and 1.35. It is indicated that the increased IG/ID value stands for enhanced graphitization degree [41]. Thus, it is obvious that R-CDs have the most graphitization degree, while B-CDs have the least. As seen in FTIR spectroscopy (Fig. 2o), three typical stretching vibrations are represented for -OH/-NH (3545–3412 cm−1), -NO2 (1620, 1403 cm−1) and C-O (1100 cm−1). The stretching vibrations of unsaturated bonds are observed, such as CH (3133 cm−1), CO (1714 cm−1) and CN (1620 cm−1), implying that these MCDs have polyaromatic structures. In particular, the peak at 1316 cm−1 corresponding to C-NC bond suggests a typical phenazine structure, further confirming the polymerization of o-PDA [42]. In addition, the intensity of these bonds increases as the color of MCDs red shifts, indicating more conjugated domains and functional groups. XPS analysis was performed and the parameters, including fitted peak position, line shape, FWHM and chemical state, are presented in Table S4-7. According to the full scan XPS spectra ( Fig. 3a-d), the four MCDs contain three distinct peaks, which respectively refer to carbon (284.8 eV), nitrogen (400.1 eV) and oxygen (531.6 eV) elements. From high-resolution XPS spectra, the C 1 s band contains sp2 carbon (CC, 284.5 eV and CO, 289.3 eV), as well as sp3 carbon (C-N, 286.1 eV and C-O, 288.4 eV). The N 1 s band contains three components, attributed to pyridinic nitrogen (398.3 eV), pyrrolic nitrogen (399.5 eV) and nitro nitrogen (405.6 eV). Moreover, the O 1 s display two peaks originated from CO and C-O with binding energies of 528.6 and 530.2 eV. In addition, for R-CDs, a small amount of Cl element (4.54%) is observed with a binding energy of 198.8 eV (Figure S5). This phenomenon can be explained that the addition of HCl leads to the little Cl element reacting on the surface of CDs [43]. To further verify the structures of MCDs, 1HNMR spectra were analyzed as shown in Figure S6. The chemical shift peaks between 7 and 8 ppm indicate the existence of aromatic structure [44]. Particularly, the peaks at 7.99 ppm and 8.28 ppm are ascribed to 2,3-diaminophenazine (2,3-DAPN) due to the self-polymerization of o-PDA. Moreover, the chemical shift peaks at 2.06, 4.44, 9.41 and 9.89 are respectively owing to OH, NH2, NH-CO and COOH, which are consistent with these results from FTIR and XPS analysis.

O-PDA is a well-known precursor to synthesize CDs with exceptional optical properties. Many researchers make great efforts to disclose the formation of CDs from o-PDA [44–47], expecting to provide a definite guide to exploring more excellent o-PDA based CDs. It is well accepted that in solvothermal or microwave synthesis, o-PDA is first dehydrated and then self-polymerized to 2,3-DAPN by means of high energy [48]. Meanwhile, 2,3-DAPN can be detected when o-PDA is dissolved in ethanol at room temperature [28]. In fact, o-PDA is a photosensitizer and can be oxidized by light or an oxidant [48]. In other words, o-PDA has the ability to self-polymerization in the conditions of room temperature and air, which provides theoretical support for CDs preparation at room temperature and in atmospheric pressure. In this study, mass spectrometry (MS) combined with structural analysis above was employed to investigate the structures of intermediates and final products (Figure S7). As seen in Fig. 4 and Table S9, a peak of m/z at 265 is present in all four MS spectra. Unlike 2,3-DAPN with m/z at 211, m/z at 265 implies a more complex intermediate product. When combined with the characterization discussed above, this compound is viewed as a 2,3-DAPN derivative ( Scheme 2). The formation processes of MCDs are shown in Fig. 4b. It is clear that the formation of the 2,3-DAPN derivative is greatly associated with the existence of DMF solvent. After self-polymerization of o-PDA by oxidation reaction, the NH2 group of 2,3-DAPN is so active that it reacts with CO group of DMF to produce CN [24]. This reaction is called Schiff base reaction and it is the reason why 2,3-DAPN derivative can’t be found using ethanol as solvent. In the case of HNO3, glucose, ascorbic acid and malic acid are oxidated and dehydrated, leading to enlarged conjugated systems. Then these products are successfully linked to the surface of the 2,3-DAPN derivative via acylation reaction. Subsequently, the energy released from oxidation, Schiff base reaction and acylation reaction further promotes dehydration, polymerization and carbonization reactions. Finally, the blue, green and yellow color fluorescence CDs are formed at room temperature and in air. As the reaction conditions above are the same, the addition of glucose, ascorbic acid and malic acid dominates the emission color of CDs by adjusting surface states and graphitization. In addition, R-CDs are generated as citric acid takes part in the reaction, using H2O2 as an oxidant. Unlike glucose, ascorbic acid and malic acid, which undergo oxidation and dehydration, citric acid is directly reacted on the surface of CDs via acylation. It is suggested that citric acid benefits to expand conjugated structures and sp2 domains, resulting in the fluorescence color of CDs red-shifted [49].

The mechanism to explain the luminescence of CDs is still ambiguous because of complex synthetic processes and subsequently unclear structures. Up to now, there are primarily two acceptable mechanisms to illustrate the fluorescence of CDs. The first is called quantum confinement effect [50,51], while the second is regarded as surface states [52–54]. The former is related to carbon cores affected by particle size and graphitization. The latter is correlated to functional groups like oxygen or nitrogen-containing groups. Both mechanisms are proposed based on the effect on band gap energy, subsequently influencing the color CDs. According to TEM images, the particle sizes of MCDs increase as the emission wavelengths shift longer, which is in good agreement with quantum confinement effect. In addition, based on Raman data analysis (Table S1), an increase IG/ID value indicates the enhancement of graphitization. Thus, it is obvious that the R-CDs have the greatest graphitization while B-CDs have the least, confirming that graphitization benefits the emission wavelengths of MCDs red shift. In a word, both particle size and graphitization play an important role in regulating fluorescence emission wavelength of MCDs. According to FTIR, XPS, 1HNMR and MS analysis, all four MCDs are doped with O and N elements and have different conjugated sp2 lengths. According to XPS data analysis (Table S2-3), the CO content rises from 16.83% in B-CDs to 32.78% in R-CDs. Meanwhile, the content of pyridine N also increases from 25.67% to 41.86% as the fluorescence color varies from blue to red. These two conjugated bonds are helpful to enhance sp2 domains and effective conjugated length, leading to emission wavelength red shifts. Because of strong electron-withdrawing functional groups, CO and pyridine N have the ability to lower the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) energy, thus reducing the bandgap and resulting in emission wavelength red shifts. Moreover, in full XPS scan spectra, it is not difficult to find that the Cl element only occurs in R-CDs with a content of 4.54%. However, it can’t be observed in MS spectra. This is due to the fact that anion fragment peaks can’t be recognized by ESI (+) mode. In terms of the synthetic process of R-CDs, the doped Cl element originates from HCl and is served as both catalyst and pH adjustor [43]. It may be explained that the electron-withdrawing functional groups, including COOH, make the H+ more positive and then easier to attract Cl- [55]. It is suggested that the presence of Cl element in CDs gives rise to a new gap state within the initial LUMO−HOMO (highest occupied molecular orbital) gap, narrowing the bandgap and then red-shifting the emission wavelength [56,57]. To shed further light on the relationship between band gap and emission wavelength, the band gap energies of the MCDs are calculated in Figure S8. The bandgap energy decreases from 2.07 eV for B-CDs to 1.59 eV for R-CDs. It agrees well with the ideal that bandgap energy adjusts the fluorescence color of MCDs. In addition, the structures of MCDs were inferred by MS combined with the characterization method discussed above. It is revealed that the R-CDs with a m/z of 637 have the largest sp2 domain. The smaller sp2 domain corresponds to the blue shift of emission wavelength, which conforms to the quantum confinement effect. The structures of MCDs were optimized by the density functional theory (DFT) method [58]. Meanwhile, LUMO and HOMO energy levels were calculated to gain the band gap. As shown in Fig. 5, the band gap narrows as the emission wavelength red shifts. The detailed data analysis indicates that the LUMO level energy decreases and the HOMO energy level increases with the emission wavelength red-shifted, leading to band gap narrowing. The results from the theoretical calculation are consistent with those from the experiment, confirming the status of the band gap in tuning color of CDs by changing particle size, graphitization, and surface states, including conjugated sp2 domains. In addition, time-resolved photoluminescence was employed to study the luminescence process (Figure S9). The values of τavg from B-CDs to R-CDs, respectively, are 1.86, 1.77, 1.14 and 0.88 ns (Table S8). In addition, τavg of B-CDs and G-CDs are biexponentially fitted with fast component τ1 and slow τ2. τ1 is assigned to the fluorescence center from core state, whereas τ2 is ascribed to surface states. For the two CDs above, the proportions of τ1 are more than 60%, indicating their fluorescence mainly originates from carbon core. On the other hand, Y-CDs and R-CDs have a single exponential decay, indicating the simple fluorescence center from core state.

Fig. 6 a shows that all four types of CDs/PVP films exhibit corresponding fluorescence colors under UV light at 365 nm. This suggests that MCDs solution could be properly dispersed in a polyvinylpyrrolidone matrix to prevent fluorescence quenching caused by π-π stacking. (Figure S10a) It is shown that the fluorescence intensity of the four MCDs/PVP films trivially fluctuated, revealing their good optical stability. From Figure S10b, the fluorescence intensities of MCDs/PVP films decrease slightly after 30 hours of irradiation by a 365 nm UV lamp, indicating their high resistance to photobleaching. Finally, the thermal stability of CDs/PVP films were analyzed in Figure S10c. As illustrated, the weights of these MCDs/PVP films reduce slightly within 105 °C, possibly caused by the evaporation of residual solvents. When the temperature increased to 394 °C, the weights quickly declined and eventually became steady, demonstrating that MCDs/PVP completely decomposed at that temperature. Based on the results above, it could be concluded that all MCDs/PVP films are highly thermally stable and have great perspective in optical devices, especially for LEDs based on optical down-conversion. Given that most LEDs working temperatures and times are below 80 °C and 24 hours, respectively, MCDs/PVP films have tremendous potential for the applications of LEDs based on optical down-conversion. Following that, CDs based monochrome LEDs are fabricated on 365 nm UV chips. The monochrome LEDs, respectively, emit blue, green, yellow, and red lights (Fig. 6b-e). The CIE color coordinates and correlated color temperature (CCT) are characterized as (0.17, 0.14) and 7402 K for blue light, (0.18, 0.35) and 5853 K for green light, (0.50, 0.49) and 3901 K for yellow light, and (0.61, 0.33) and 1936 K for red light, respectively. Thus, it is shown that these MCDs are suitable to be applied in down-converting LEDs preparation.

In summary, the synthesis of MCDs at room temperature and in atmosphere was performed in this study. Using o-PDA as a precursor, DMF as solvent and HNO3 as an oxidant, the presence of glucose, ascorbic acid and malic acid, respectively, produce blue, green and yellow color CDs. Meanwhile, employing H2O2 as an oxidant, combined with citric acid, red color emission CDs are successfully prepared. The QYs of B-CDs, G-CDs, Y-CDs and R-CDs are 32.3%, 26.8%, 22.6% and 13.5%. The formation mechanism of MCDs was investigated by structural characterization. It is revealed that the oxidation, Schiff base and amidation reactions provide sufficient energy to support the dehydration, polymerization and carbonation of precursors, hence promoting the formation of MCDs. The optical mechanism was disclosed based on both experimental and theoretical analyses. It is shown that larger particle sizes, more strengthen graphitization and more extended conjugated system contribute to band gap narrowing, further leading to the emission wavelength red-shifted. Finally, the MCDs composed with PVP are successfully applied in down-converting LEDs fabrication with satisfying optical property. It is indicated that the MCDs have a high potential for down-converting LEDs applications. This study proposes a new approach for CDs preparation at room temperature and in atmosphere, which is more energy-saving, convenient and safe. In addition to applying the idea of “energy-efficient chemistry”, this method potentially implements the CDs in large-scale production.

Du Pengfei: Formal analysis. Shen Jie: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Xu Guihua: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization. Zheng Xujing: Formal analysis, Data curation. Lin Liangliang: Resources, Methodology, Investigation. Xu Hujun: Funding acquisition, Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Xu Xuechun: Formal analysis, Data curation. Zhang Liguo: Data curation, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We warmly acknowledge support from National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 52004102, 22078125), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20190605).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2024.133486.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.
